segments.s126-g1: غمی is the head noun 'a grief' and the following words identify whose grief it is: بد دل functions as an attributive phrase 'ill‑hearted / heavy‑hearted' describing the king, and شاه هاماوران is the noun phrase 'the king of Hamavaran'. In classical order the possessor phrase follows the possessed noun, yielding 'the grief of the ill‑hearted king of Hamavaran.'
segments.s126-g2: ز هرگونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'by/in every kind / in every way'; چاره is the noun 'remedy/solution'; جست is the past form 'sought' (here 'he sought'); اندرَان is an adverbial 'therein / among those means' linking back to the kinds just mentioned. Together: 'sought a remedy therein/in every way.'
segments.s126-g3: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; یک هفته 'one week'; بگذشت is the past verb 'had passed' (time passed); هشتم پگاه is an ordinal time expression 'the eighth dawn / on the eighth morning' — classical ordering places the ordinal before the time noun.
segments.s126-g4: فرستاده is the noun 'a messenger' (agent); آمد is the simple past 'came'; به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' indicating movement toward; شاه is the indirect object 'the king'. Word order is subject (messenger) + verb + directional phrase + goal.
segments.s126-g5: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; بیند is the present/subjunctive form 'sees' (classical third person); the second که introduces the content clause 'that'; مهمان خویش is 'his guest' where خویش is built from خوی + possessive ش (his own). The whole segment is the protasis 'if the king sees that his guest...'.
segments.s126-g6: بیاید here is a present/subjunctive sense 'may come / comes' used for an event seen as forthcoming; خرامان is an adverb/advective adjective 'with a stately/proud gait'; به ایوان خویش is the directional phrase 'to his portico/porch' (ایوان) with the possessive formed again by خوی + ش. Together: 'is coming stately to his portico.'
segments.s126-g7: شود is the 3rd singular present/future 'becomes / will be'; شهر هاماوران is the noun phrase 'the city of Hamavaran' (place name following شهر); ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed/precious' that completes the copular idea 'the city will become esteemed.'
segments.s126-g8: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بینند is 3rd person plural present 'they see' (subject unspecified, impersonal 'people'); رخشنده‌گاه is a compound noun 'place of radiance' and بلند is the adjective 'lofty/high' modifying it. The clause means 'when they see the lofty radiant place.'
segments.s126-g97: چرا introduces a question ('why'); روز جنگش is a genitival phrase 'the day of his battle' (روز + جنگ + possessive -ش). نکردند is past tense, 3rd person plural negative 'they did not do'; بند is the object 'a fetter/bond' (word order is inverted from colloquial حمزه: expected بند نکردند).
segments.s126-g98: که here gives the reason ('for/since'); جامه‌اش is 'his garment' (possessive -اش); زره بود is a simple past copula clause 'was armor'; و links the two predicates; تختش سمند is a verbless clause equating his seat/throne with a steed (poetic apposition).
segments.s126-g99: سپهدار is the noun 'commander'; چون here means 'like' and introduces a comparison; the proper names follow in a simple list (گیو و گودرز و طوس) in apposition to سپهدار.
segments.s126-g100: بدرید is a past transitive verb 'they tore' (3rd pl.); دلتان is the object 'your heart(s)' with the plural possessive -تان; ز introduces cause/agent 'by/from' in poetic usage; آوای + کوس is a genitive construction 'the sound/voice of the drum' (کوس = war‑drum).
segments.s126-g101: همی is an older/dialectal adverb meaning 'always/continually' modifying the verb; تخت زرین is a noun phrase 'the golden throne' (adjective after noun is normal in Persian poetry); کمینگه کنید is an instruction ('make an ambush' or 'set as an ambush') where کنید is 2nd‑person plural/formal imperative/subjunctive 'do/make'.
segments.s126-g102: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic); پیوستگی دست is a noun phrase 'the joining/connectedness of hands' (پیوستگی = unity/connectedness, دست = hands or joined hands); کوته کنید is again an instruction 'make short / cut off' (metaphorically 'sever' or 'break apart').
segments.s126-g103: فرستادگان را is the definite direct object marked by را ('the envoys'); سگان is a pejorative noun 'dogs' used here as an insult (figuratively 'scoundrels'); کرد is past 'he made/did' used with نام ('name') to mean 'he named/called them'; the word order is poetic/inverted compared with colloquial 'فرستادگان را سگان نام کرد'.
segments.s126-g104: همی again marks continuous action 'continually'; ریخت here is the sense 'pouring/flow' (past of ریختن) 'poured'; خونابه is 'gore, blood'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; گل is 'flower' (poetically often used for the fallen or for beauty); مدام is an adverb 'constantly/continually' reinforcing همی.
segments.s126-g121: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The phrase pairs two sources: 'ترکان' (the Turks) and 'از دشت نیزه‌وران' ('from the plain, the spearmen'); word order is coordinate: 'ز ترکان و از دشت نیزه‌وران' = 'from the Turks and from the plain (the spearmen)'.
segments.s126-g122: ز هر سو is 'from every side' (هر سو = each side); بیامد is simple past 'came' (poetic past). سپاهی گران is a noun phrase where گران 'heavy/worthy' qualifies سپاهی 'host/army' — literally 'a heavy/weighty host' arrived.
segments.s126-g123: گران لشکری: گران 'mighty/heavy' modifies لشکری (troops, soldiery); ساخت is the simple past 'made/formed' with the object 'لشکری' and subject 'افراسیاب' (name placed after the verb, a common poetic order).
segments.s126-g124: برآمد is past 'rose/stood up'. سر از ... برآمدن is an idiom meaning 'to lift one's head out of / rise from' (i.e., stop eating or sleeping and get up); here the verb is fronted for poetic word order, and the list خورد و آرام و خواب enumerates the activities he rose from: eating, rest/repose, and sleep.
segments.s126-g125: از ایران برآمد 'rose from Iran' — برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. ز هر سو خروش follows: ز هر سو 'on every side' and خروش 'a cry/uproar' (collective tumult) — the clause describes widespread outcry originating in Iran.
segments.s126-g126: شد is the past of 'to become' (became). آرام گیتی is 'the calm/peaceful world' (آرام qualifies گیتی). پر از جنگ‌وجوش means 'full of war-and-fervor' — پر 'full (of)' + از introduces the content, and جنگ‌وجوش is a compound meaning 'war and commotion/strife'.
segments.s126-g127: برآشفت is a past verb meaning 'became angry/was agitated'. 'افراسیاب آن زمان' places the proper name before the time expression; 'آن زمان' = 'at that time' (آن = 'then/that', زمان = 'time').
segments.s126-g128: برآویخت is past 'rose up/sprung up' (a sudden rising to action). با + لشکر تازیان means 'with the army of the Taziān' (تازیان here = Arabs); با marks accompaniment or means 'with'.
segments.s126-g81: This noun phrase is fronted and marked as a definite object by را: همان مهتران دگر را ‘those same other chiefs’. به بند is a prepositional phrase (‘into/into bonds’) that prepares the result; the main verb (فگند) appears in the following segment. Note the archaic/orderly presentation: object + goal before the verb.
segments.s126-g82: فگند is an older/polite past of فکند ‘threw/ cast’ and is the main verb of the sentence, placed at the end. در دژ is the locative (‘into/inside the fortress’). شاه کاووس is the (accusative) person thrown into the fortress. The word ابا here is irregular/archaic in this context (apparently functioning like a conjunction or connective ‘and/into’); its exact lexical sense is not given in the provided sense list.
segments.s126-g83: ز is the poetic form of از ‘from/of’; here it introduces the group whose number is given. گردان نگهبان دژ is a genitive sequence ‘the troop/contingent of the fortress-guards’. شد هزار uses شد ‘became’ as a past existential/quantifying verb: ‘numbered/became a thousand’. Note verb-final order.
segments.s126-g84: This is an appositive/descriptor for the thousand: همه نامداران خنجر‌گذار ‘all renowned (men), dagger-bearers’. همه + noun = ‘all/the whole’, and نامداران modifies the following compound noun خنجر‌گذار (‘those who carry daggers’).
segments.s126-g85: سراپرده او is a possessive noun phrase ‘his inner tent/inner chamber’. به تاراج داد is an idiom ‘gave up to pillage/left to be plundered’, with داد as the past transitive verb ‘gave’. Word order puts the possessed thing before the verbal phrase.
segments.s126-g86: به پرمایگان is the indirect object ‘to/for the great ones (nobles)’. بدره و تاج are the direct objects given to them; داد is the past verb ‘gave’. بدره is a poetic item here (likely a garment/ornament) but the provided senses do not list that meaning, so its exact lexical sense is noted as unspecified.
segments.s126-g87: برفتند is the past plural verb ‘they departed/went forth’. پوشیده رویان is a descriptive phrase ‘those with covered faces’ (pūshīde = covered); دو خیل follows to give quantity ‘two hosts/squadrons’. Typical Persian word order places the verb before its descriptive/quantifying noun phrases in older poetic style.
segments.s126-g88: This is a compressed clause giving a notable individual among the ranks: عماری (a proper name or identifier) + یکی ‘one’ + درمیانش ‘among them’ + جلیل ‘venerable/majestic’. The order is inverted for emphasis: ‘one Amari among them was venerable.’ عماری here functions as a name/identifier (sense not listed in the provided choices).
segments.s126-g89: 'که' here introduces a subordinate/result clause ('that/so'); 'سودابه را' marks Sudāba as the definite direct object with 'را'; 'باز جای آورند' is a verbal phrase in the narrative past (آورند = 'they brought/placed'), literally 'brought back into place' with the subject 'they' left implicit at the verb's end.
segments.s126-g90: 'سراپرده را' is the definite object 'the inner curtain/inner chamber' marked by 'را'; the phrase 'زیر پای آورند' is an idiom ('bring under the foot') meaning 'trample' or 'humiliate', with آورند again the past narrative verb 'they brought' and the agent 'they' omitted.
segments.s126-g91: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; the order is 'چو + subject (سودابه) + object (پوشیدگان را) + verb (بدید)', so 'When Sudāba saw the veiled ones.' 'پوشیدگان' is a participial noun 'the covered/veiled (women)' and 'را' marks it as definite object; 'بدید' is the past-tense verb 'saw'.
segments.s126-g92: The prepositional pair 'ز بر' means 'from off/away from' (poetic 'ز' + 'بر' = 'off of/from upon'); 'جامه خسروی' is 'the kingly/royal garment' (خسروی qualifying جامه). 'بردرید' is the past verb 'she/they tore off' (بردرید = tore apart/off). The verb at the end takes the preceding phrase as its object.
segments.s126-g93: 'به' here marks direction/attachment ('onto/at'); 'مشکین کمند' is a compound 'musk‑hued / dark noose/lasso'; 'اندرآویخت' is the past verb 'grappled/engaged in wrestling' and 'چنگ' (placed after the verb) is the agent/subject 'the claw (or talon)'. The word order (verb before subject) is typical of poetic narrative.
segments.s126-g94: The phrase begins with 'به فندق' ('on/to the hazel' — here 'فندق' = hazel/ hazel-tree), then 'گلان را' is the definite object 'the blossoms/flowers' marked by 'را'. 'به خون داد رنگ' is an idiomal sequence: 'داد' (gave) with 'رنگ' (color) and the instrumental 'به خون' ('with blood') — together 'gave color with blood' = 'stained/blew red with blood.'
segments.s126-g95: 'بدیشان' ('to them') is a prepositional phrase indicating the addressees; 'چنین' is the adverb 'thus' or 'in this way'; 'گفت' is the speech verb 'said' introducing the reported clause beginning with 'کاین' ('that this'); 'کارکرد' is the noun 'deed/action' that the reported clause will comment on. Note: the senses list for 'گفت' supplied in the prompt does not include the expected 'said', so its sense index is given as -1.
segments.s126-g96: 'ستوده ندارند' functions as 'do not regard as praised/they do not praise' — ستوده 'praised' and ندارند 'they do not have/they do not hold (it so)' with negation; the subject 'مردان مرد' follows the verb (poetic word order) and is an emphatic plural 'men of men' = 'true/valiant men'. Together: 'true men do not praise (this).'
segments.s126-g65: بود here is past existence ('there was'); نیز is an adverb 'also'; پیوسته modifies خونی as an adjectival phrase 'continuous/steady'; خونی is a noun/adjective 'of blood / blood-red' describing the flush; که introduces a relative clause linking this blood/flush to what follows ("that..."); مهر is the head of the relative clause meaning 'love/affection'.
segments.s126-g66: ببرد is a present/subjunctive-like form 'would take / carry away' (verb برد used here); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'; تا introduces a limit 'until'; بگرددت is a verbal form meaning 'it turns / it is turned' (here 'until it changes'); چهر is the object 'face/appearance' that is affected by that turning.
segments.s126-g67: چو is a conditional 'if/when' here meaning 'if'; مهر کسی را بخواهی is a subordinate clause 'if you want someone's love' (کسی را with را marks the definite object); بخواهی is present/subjunctive/optative 'you wish/desire'; ستود follows as an imperative/infinitival notion 'praise' or 'one should praise' — in poetry the verb can present an admonition connected to the condition.
segments.s126-g68: بباید is a modal 'one must / ought to'; به introduces the domain 'by/with'; سود و زیان are the paired nouns 'profit (benefit) and loss'; آزمود is a verbal adjective/past participle 'tested/tried' used as the verb 'to try/test'—together the clause means 'you must test by benefit and loss'.
segments.s126-g69: پسر‌گر is a classical compound with -گر functioning as a conditional/agentive element here ('if a son' / 'when a son'); به جاه 'in rank/status' (به + noun); از تو برتر شود is a clause 'becomes superior to you' with از marking the comparison and شود as the present/future 'become'.
segments.s126-g70: هم 'also' links this result to the previous clause; از رشک مهر تو 'from (because of) jealousy/envy of your affection' uses از meaning 'from/because of'; لاغر شود is a result clause 'becomes thin / grows emaciated' with شود as the copular/auxiliary verb.
segments.s126-g71: چنین است is the copular phrase 'thus it is'; گیهان 'the world' is the subject; ناپاک رای is a noun phrase 'impure opinion/counsel' with ناپاک qualifying رای — together they name the worldly disposition being described.
segments.s126-g72: به هر باد 'at/by every wind' (به + هر + باد); خیره بجنبد zجمبد is a verbal phrase where خیره 'gazing/stunned' describes the manner and بجنبد 'is moved/starts to move' is the verb; ز جای 'from its place' (ز = from, جای = place) gives the result/location displaced.
segments.s126-g41: به + noun phrase gives a locative/into reading: 'به کاخ اندرون' = 'into / in the inside of the palace'. Persian places adjectives after the noun, so 'تخت زرین' = 'throne (that is) golden'. نهاد is the simple past (3rd person) of نهادن (to place/put) and here means 'he placed/put'. The whole segment thus states the action of setting a golden throne inside the palace.
segments.s126-g42: نشست is past (3rd sing.) of نشستن, 'he sat (down)'. The classical phrase از بر + تخت gives a locative 'on/upon the throne' (older style combining از and بر); کاووس is the possessor ('Kavus’s throne'), and شاد is a predicative adjective 'happy/joyful' describing his state as he sat. Word order is typical older Persian: verb then locative phrase then adjective-state.
segments.s126-g43: همی here is the archaic/progressive marker used with past verbs meaning something like 'he was / he kept on being'. بود is the past of بودن 'was'; یک هفته gives duration 'for a week'. با + noun ('با می‌به دست') is instrumental/with: 'with wine in hand' — می‌به is a poetic/old term for a wine-related drink and دست is literal 'hand' used idiomatically ('with X in hand').
segments.s126-g44: خوش و خرم are two adjectives placed together ('pleasant and joyful') functioning adverbially/predicatively. آمدش is past 'came to him' (the -ش = 'to him'); جای نشست is a nominal phrase 'the place/seat of sitting' (جای = place; نشست used as a noun/nominalized idea of 'a sitting/seat'). The construction آمدش جایِ ... means 'a ... place/seat came to him' — i.e. he obtained/was given a pleasant, joyful seat.
segments.s126-g45: شب و روز = 'night and day' (continuous time adverb). بر پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before (them)' or 'in the presence of'; چون introduces a comparison 'like / as'. کهتران is an archaic plural meaning 'the elders / the older men' (classical usage) and functions as the term of comparison ('like the elders').
segments.s126-g46: میان here means 'the middle / centre' and is the element being 'closed' or 'blocked'. بسته is the past of بستن 'shut/close' and is used VSO-style with the subject following it (typical of older/epic Persian). بد is an adjective 'bad/wicked' modifying شاه 'king', and هاماوران is a descriptive noun 'the (flat) plains' or a place-name-like epithet attached to شاه ('king of the plains'). The clause thus says that the wicked king (of the plains) shut/blocked the middle.
segments.s126-g47: ببسته is a past form of بستن (here 'he enclosed / he set / he marshalled'), with verb first as in older style. همه لشکرش را is the definite direct object marked by را: 'all his army'. میان is the locative 'in the middle / center' specifying where he placed/enclosed them. The sequence V + object + locative is a common older Persian order.
segments.s126-g48: پرستنده is a present-participle/agentive 'worshipping / paying homage (to)' or 'revering' and here functions adverbially/qualifying the manner. بر پیش = 'in the presence of / before', and ایرانیان is the group 'the Iranians'. The phrase says that this was done 'reverently / as if worshipping before the Iranians' (i.e., in the sight/presence of the Iranians).
segments.s126-g129: Literal word order is somewhat inverted: the locative phrase 'به جنگ اندرون' ('into/within the war') precedes the verb 'بود' and the subject 'لشکر'. 'سه ماه' is a duration placed after the subject and verb meaning 'for three months'. 'بود' is simple past copula meaning 'remained/was'.
segments.s126-g130: 'بدادند' is a past plural verb 'they gave/handed over'. 'سرها' (plural) is the direct object 'heads'. The prepositional phrase 'ز بهر کلاه' functions as 'for the sake of helmets' (z + baher = 'because/for'), so the clause means they delivered heads for helmets (trophy/spoils).
segments.s126-g131: 'چنین است' is a fixed pattern meaning 'thus it is'. 'رسم' is the noun 'custom' and functions as the subject/predicate complement after 'است'. 'سرای سپنج' is a genitive phrase 'house (سرای) of the sponge (سپنج)' — a poetic epithet for the world/abode; the lemma senses provided do not include the noun 'house', so pick -1 for that word's listed senses.
segments.s126-g132: This is a balanced antithesis: 'گهی ... و گهی ...' means 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. 'ناز و نوش' are paired pleasures ('naaz' = pampering/caress, 'nush' = delight/pleasure). 'درد و رنج' are the paired sorrows ('dard' as affliction/pain, 'ranj' as suffering). The conjunctions 'و' simply link the nouns.
segments.s126-g133: 'سرانجام' is an adverb meaning 'in the end' and modifies the verb 'بگذرد'. The coordinated phrase 'نیک و بدش' ('good and bad') shares the possessive clitic 'ش' ('his/its'), so it reads 'his good and bad'. 'بگذرد' is third person singular present/past-finite form meaning 'passes' or 'comes to an end'.
segments.s126-g134: 'شکارست' is a nominal predicate meaning 'is prey / is a target'. 'مرگش' is a possessed noun 'his death'. 'همی' is an adversative/adverbial particle here meaning 'indeed/still' that accompanies the verb. 'بشکرد' is an archaic/poetic verb; in this context it means 'comes to pass' (i.e., his death will occur).
segments.s126-g135: 'شکست آمد' is an idiom meaning 'defeat came' or 'a defeat occurred'. The source agent is marked by 'از ترک' ('from the Turk(s)') and the affected party by 'بر تازیان' ('upon the Arabs' / 'against the Arabs'), so the clause means that the Turks caused a defeat for the Arabs.
segments.s126-g136: 'ز بهر فزونی' = 'because of/in view of (their) superiority/greater numbers' (z + baher gives a causal sense). 'سرآمد' here functions as a resultive predicate meaning 'resulted' or 'came about' but the provided lemma senses don't include that verbal/result sense, so mark it -1. 'زیان' is the resulting noun 'loss/damage'.
segments.s126-g145: دریغ‌ست is a fixed poetic exclamation meaning “alas/it is a pity” with the clitic copula -ست. ایران is the subject (land) and که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. ویران is a predicative adjective 'ruined', and شود is the present subjunctive / potential of شدن ‘to become’, giving the sense 'that it should become ruined.' The clause is elliptical in Persian style (copula and some linking are compressed).
segments.s126-g146: کنام is a noun meaning 'bosom/lair/seat of' (here 'the dwelling of'). It takes the following nouns as its possessors. پلنگان و شیران are coordinated nouns 'leopards and lions' (literal wild animals here). شود repeats the verb 'become' and links back to the previous clause: 'and (it) becomes the lair of...'. Word order is noun phrase (کنام + possessor) followed by the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s126-g147: This compact segment is elliptical: همه جای جنگی literally 'every place (is) warlike / a place of battle' with جنگی as a postpositive adjective/attributive. سواران بدی follows as a nominal phrase 'the horsemen are bad/evil' where بدی is a predicate/adjective following the noun, a normal Persian order (noun + adjective/predicate). The copula 'is/are' is omitted (understood).
segments.s126-g148: نشستنگه is an older/poetic noun meaning 'seat; place to sit' (i.e., throne/seat). شهریاران is the plural 'kings' (owner/possessors of such seats). بدی again functions as a predicate 'is bad/evil' referring to those seats or their condition; the clause is nominal and copula-less, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s126-g149: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. جای سختی و رنج و بلاست is a noun phrase 'a place of hardship and suffering and calamity' with the final -ست (copula) attached to بلا to yield 'is calamity' (poetic compression). The list of nouns is linked by و 'and'. The overall clause states the present condition: 'now (this) is a place of hardship...'.
segments.s126-g150: نشستنگه again 'the seat/place of sitting' is followed by the compound modifier تیزچنگ اژدهاست: تیزچنگ is a descriptive compound 'sharp-clawed' (or a name), and اژدهاست combines 'dragon' with the copula -ست, yielding 'is the dragon's seat' or 'the seat belongs to the sharp-clawed dragon.' The structure is noun (نشستنگه) + possessive/identifying noun phrase + copula.
segments.s126-g151: کسی کز ... شیر is a conditional/relative clause: کسی 'someone' + کز (archaic contraction of که + ز) 'who/from whom' introduces the relative 'who has been taken/by whom...'. پلنگان is the agent 'leopards'. بخوردست is an older/perhaps passive or resultative form 'has been eaten/taken' (here 'taken/attacked by'), and شیر that follows is the main noun of the relative clause continued into the main clause (the syntax is compressed: 'anyone whom the leopards have taken — the lion ...').
segments.s126-g152: بدین رنج ما را بود دستگیر is the result/main clause: بدین 'by/with this' (classical prepositional particle), رنج 'suffering', ما را 'to us' (object/beneficiary with the Persian object marker را), بود is the copula 'would be/was' (here conditional/potential sense), and دستگیر is a substantive/adjective 'helper/succorer'. The order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + indirect object + copula + predicate noun.
segments.s126-g73: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. The clause uses the idiom 'بر + noun + بسته شدن' (here 'بر خیرگی بسته شد') to mean 'to be seized by/overcome with [something]'; 'بسته شد' is past tense (شد = became) indicating a completed state: 'was seized/struck by astonishment.' Word order is classical/poetic (prepositional phrase after the subject is normal).
segments.s126-g74: 'به' introduces the target/party to which the opinion attaches ('to the plains/plainsmen'). 'رای پیوسته شد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'the counsel/opinion became joined' or 'the council sided (with...)' — 'پیوسته' is a past-participial/adjectival form 'joined/connected' and 'شد' (past) marks the result. The whole means 'the council came to be attached to the plains (i.e., sided with them).'
segments.s126-g75: 'یکی' here = 'a certain/one (there was one)'; 'کوه بودش' = 'there was a mountain' with the enclitic '-ش' on 'بود' referring back to the mountain ('it was'); 'سر اندر سحاب' places the summit 'in the clouds' (اندر = in). The clause is a descriptive verbless/compound description typical of epic style: 'one (thing) was a mountain, (its) head in the clouds.'
segments.s126-g76: 'برآورده' is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'raised/brought up'; placed before 'ایزد' it indicates the agent ('raised by God') in a concise epic syntax (no explicit passive auxiliary is needed). 'از قعر آب' gives the origin: 'out of/from the depths of the water.' Together: '(it was) raised by God from the bottom/depths of the waters.'
segments.s126-g77: This is a noun phrase: یکی ('a certain/one') + دژ ('fortress') + برآورده (a past participle/adjective 'raised, erected') followed by the source phrase از کوهسار ('from/of the mountainside'). In classical Persian adjectives or participial modifiers often follow the noun they modify, so برآورده describes دژ; از introduces the place from which it rises.
segments.s126-g78: تو گفتی is the second-person past 'you said', introducing reported speech. سپهرستش is a contracted/clitic form of سپهر استِش (سپهر + است + -ش) meaning 'its sky/firmament is'; the enclitic -ش (= 'its') refers back to the fortress. اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at/alongside' (اندر = in/at; کنار = side/beside) and completes the reported clause: 'you said its firmament was at its side / beside the sky.'
segments.s126-g79: بدان is the compound/demonstrative/directional 'to that' (here given as ب + دان); دژ is the destination 'fortress'. فرستاد is the past tense verb 'sent' (third-person, subject omitted). کاووس را is the object with the definite-object marker را ('Kavus' as the person sent). The line places the destination before the verb, a common word order in Persian, and the object after the verb marked by را.
segments.s126-g80: همان here functions as 'the same/that (aforementioned)' or as an emphatic demonstrative introducing the list of persons. گیو و گودرز ('Giv and Goudarz') are coordinated names; و is the conjunction 'and'. هم طوس را adds 'and also Tous' with هم = 'also' and the final را marking the coordinated names collectively as the objects of فرستاد. In classical Persian the object-marker را may appear once at the end of a coordinated series.
segments.s126-g25: The clause gives the city's existence and name. یکی شهر = “a certain city” (یکی as indefinite). شاه را uses the object/definite marker را after شهر (here marking the city as the item being named), and شاهه نام is an archaic, condensed predicative phrase meaning “was named Shahah” (نام used as the copular/predicative element: ‘named’). Word order is topical/poetic: indefinite + noun + را + name + نام.
segments.s126-g26: همه از در introduces the scene around the city gate: همه 'everyone' + از در 'at/from the gate' (در here = gate/door). جشن و سور و خرام is a triad of celebratory nouns/verbs: جشن (festivity), سور (banquet), خرام (proud striding/processional gait). The list is paratactic (ands connect the nouns) and describes what everyone was doing at the gate.
segments.s126-g27: بدان شهر is 'in that city' (بدان = به + آن in older/formal diction). بودش is the past copula with enclitic -ش 'his/there' (it was / there was his), so بودش سرای و نشست = 'there was his sarā (palace) and a نشست (gathering/session)'. Word order places the locative/contrastive phrase first (بدان شهر) as is typical in epic diction.
segments.s126-g28: همه شهر سرتاسر is a fronted quantifying phrase meaning 'the whole city, throughout/from end to end'. آذین ببست is a light-verb construction: آذین (decoration) + ببست (past of بستن here 'to tie/fasten' = 'they adorned/tied up'); together: 'the whole city was decked/was adorned throughout.' The subject is topicalized, and the verb is past tense.
segments.s126-g29: چو = 'when'; در شاهه شد is a temporal clause meaning 'when he came/was at the gate/threshold of Shahah' (در = gate/door; شاهه here is the place-name/palace). شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/was/arrived' used intransitively to mark the action of arriving/being there. شاه گردن‌فراز follows as a nominative apposition/description: 'the (proud) king' (گردن‌فراز an epithet 'proud/boastful').
segments.s126-g30: همه شهر is the agent/subject 'the whole city'. بردند is the past-tense verb 'they carried/brought' and پیشش is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before him/in his presence' (the clitic -ش = 'him'). نماز here means 'prayer/obeisance' — in epic diction it can mean ritual homage rather than strictly Islamic prayer. The clause means 'the whole city brought/presented prayers before him.'
segments.s126-g31: همه گوهر و زعفران ریختند is a simple past clause: همه 'all' + گوهر و زعفران 'gems and saffron' as direct objects, and ریختند (past of ریختن) 'they poured/scattered/offered'. In epic lists like this ریختند often conveys lavish offering or scattering.
segments.s126-g32: به دینار و عنبر برآمیختند uses به + nouns to indicate what they mixed/combined: به دینار و عنبر = 'dinar-coins and amber', and برآمیختند (past of برآمیختن) = 'they mingled / blended together'. The verb is transitive plural past, continuing the series of lavish gifts; word order places the items before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s126-g49: بدین‌گونه is an adverb meaning 'thus' and sets the manner. تا here introduces a resultant extent ('so that' / 'to the point that'); یکسر is an adverbial intensifier 'entirely/wholly'. ایمن شدند is past tense (شند/شدند) of شدن with a plural subject: 'they became safe' (شدند as a lexical past of شدن, not an auxiliary here). Word order places the manner/result phrase before the predicate in a poetic style.
segments.s126-g50: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (indicating source/protection from). چون و چرا is the idiomatic pair meaning questioning/objections ('how'/'why' in the sense of objections). نهیب is a noun here meaning a rebuke or harsh cry, and گزند means 'harm' or 'injury'. The conjunction و links the listed dangers from which they became safe.
segments.s126-g51: همه is the subject 'everyone/all'. گفته بودند is a past perfect/pluperfect-like construction: گفته (past participle) + بودند (here functioning as auxiliary) = 'had said' / 'had spoken'. و is the conjunction. آراسته is an adjective/predicate 'arrayed/ornamented' used predicatively (the copula 'were' is omitted as a literary ellipsis, understood from بودند).
segments.s126-g52: سگالیده is a past-participial adjective meaning 'set upon/pursued' (or 'mauled by dogs' in a literal reading); it describes a state. از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (their) place(s)', and برخاسته (past participle of برخاستن) means 'risen'/'sprung up'. Together they mean '(those) set upon rose up from their places.'
segments.s126-g53: ز is the preposition 'about/from' (poetic variant of از). بربر here means 'barbarian(s)' (not specifically the North African Berbers in this context). برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner/thus'. آگه شدند is past tense ('they became aware' / 'they learned of'). The clause therefore means 'they learned of the barbarians in this way.'
segments.s126-g54: سگالش is a rare/archaic term (the gloss given is uncertain); here it functions as a noun meaning something like 'their tumult/assault/commotion' (contextual interpretation). چنین بود is the simple past 'was thus/was like this.' همره (variant of همراه) means 'with/together', and شدند ('they became') here yields 'they joined together' or 'they accompanied (one another)' — a poetic way to say they united or accompanied.
segments.s126-g55: شبی is an adverbial temporal phrase 'one night' or 'by night'. بانگ بوق is a noun phrase 'the sound/cry of the horn/trumpet' and آمد is the past 'came/sounded' (the horn sounded). و is the conjunction. تاختن here is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to charge' (or 'the charging') linked to the previous action: the trumpet sounded and (the call to) charge followed.
segments.s126-g56: کسی را with را marks the affected person ('anyone' as object/topic). نبد is the negative existential 'was not / did not exist' (used impersonally here). آرزو is the noun 'desire/wish'. ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/do' (used here in a light verbal sense). The clause reads idiomatically as 'there was not a man who did not desire it' — i.e., no one lacked the wish to charge.
segments.s126-g161: پس is a temporal/conjunctive adverb 'then'. آگاهی is the noun 'tidings/information' and آمد is the simple past 3rd-person 'came' (word-order here places the verbal phrase before the source). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the source, and کاووس شاه is a name + title ('Kavus the king') following the preposition. Together the phrase means 'then tidings came from King Kavus.'
segments.s126-g162: This prepositional phrase (introduced by ز) supplies the content/topic of the tidings. بند here is the noun 'bond/restraint' (not the poetic sense 'stanza'), modifying or linked to کمین‌گاه 'ambush-site' (the two form a phrase 'بندِ کمین‌گاه' = 'the ambush's restraint' or 'the restraint of the ambush'). و connects a parallel noun phrase کار سپاه 'the army's affair/operation' (کار governing سپاه as a genitive relationship). The whole segment is therefore 'about the ambush's restraint and the army's affairs.'
segments.s126-g163: سپه را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the army' as object). یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' modifying the verb. ز کابل is the source ('from Kabul'). بخواند is the past 3rd-person form of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/summon' (not 'to read'). Word order places the object before adverbial phrases for emphasis but the verb governs the whole action.
segments.s126-g164: میان بسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'having closed the middle' (میان 'middle' + بسته past-participle 'closed'), functioning adverbially to describe how the troops were arranged. بر جنگ uses بر with the sense 'to/toward (battle)' and introduces the purpose/location ('into battle'). و links the following clause: لشکر براند where براند is past 3rd-person of راندن 'to drive/lead' meaning 'he drove/led the host.' The overall sense: 'having closed the center, he marshaled them into battle and drove the host.'
segments.s126-g57: ز بربرستان is a prepositional phrase 'from Barbarestan' (ز = poetic 'from'); چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when/once' and is followed by the past verb بیامد 'came'. سپاه is the subject placed after the verb — poetic inversion (V–S) is common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s126-g58: به هاماوران is a directional phrase 'to/into the Hamavaran plains' (به = toward/into). شاددل is a predicate adjective 'joyful'; گشت (past of گشتن) functions as a copular verb 'became'. شاه is the subject placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s126-g59: گرفتند is a 3rd-person-plural past tense 'they seized/captured'. ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly' that modifies the verb. کاووس is the definite direct object marked by را which follows the object in Persian.
segments.s126-g60: چو here is used in an enumerative sense 'as/also' to list additional objects: چو گودرز و چون گیو و چون طوس — 'Goudarz and Giv and Tous'. The final را attaches to the last proper name and covers the whole coordinated series as the definite object. طوس here is the hero Tous (not the place).
segments.s126-g61: چو is the conjunction 'when'. گوید is the (poetic/historic) present/presentive form 'says/speaks' introducing reported speech; درین is the contraction of در + این 'in these' (spelled together in poetry). مردم پیش‌بین is the noun phrase 'the people's seer' (پیش‌بین = foreseer).
segments.s126-g62: چه دانی is a present interrogative 'what do you know'. تو‌ای is a vocative 'you, O ...' (poetic), and کاردان is a vocative epithet 'skilled/expert'. اندرین means 'in this / regarding this' and links back to the seer's statement.
segments.s126-g63: چو here means 'if/when'. پیوسته modifies خون as 'continuous/continually' (پیوسته خون نباشد = 'there is no continual bloodshed'). نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form 'is not' in this subordinate clause. کسی functions as 'anyone' in this negative/conditional context.
segments.s126-g64: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one should not / it ought not'. برو بودن is a verbal phrase where برو (here) combines with بودن to mean 'to go on being / to remain' (poetic phrasing). ایمن بسی is a predicative phrase 'very/safely' with بسی adding emphasis; together the clause means 'one should not remain (so) safe' or 'one should not consider oneself safe.'
segments.s126-g17: ترا (ت + را) is the object 'you' (second-person suffix + object marker) placed before the verb, a normal literary order. بی‌بهانه is an adverbial phrase 'without cause / unprovoked' modifying the verb phrase. به چنگ آوردند is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to seize/capture' (به + noun چنگ 'claw' + آوردند); here آورند is the classical 3pl present/subjunctive/jussive form used with the following prohibition (it reports the action they might do).
segments.s126-g18: نباید is an impersonal modal/prohibitive 'must not / should not'. که introduces the subordinate clause ('that...'). با + سور is a prepositional phrase 'with / against Sur' (Sūr here read as the party/side), جنگ آورند is another idiomatic verbal sequence 'to wage war' (جنگ + آوردن); آورند again is the 3pl verbal form describing the prohibited action.
segments.s126-g19: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of / because of'; here it frames the purpose/benefit. منست is an archaic predicate meaning roughly 'is mine / concerns me' or 'is for me' (the phrase together: 'this whole conversation is for my sake'). این همه گفت‌وگوی is the noun phrase 'this whole conversation' (این = this, همه = whole/all, گفت‌وگوی = conversation).
segments.s126-g20: ترا (ت + را) again is the object 'you'. زین شدن انده is a nominalized event: زین شدن 'to be saddled / to mount the saddle' with انده (a past-participial/relational element 'placed / having been put') giving the sense 'the placing/saddling of you'. آید is the present form 'comes' (here idiomatically 'brings / causes to appear') and به‌روی 'on the face(s) of / before' indicates where the sorrow appears: so together 'your saddling brings sorrow upon their faces.'
segments.s126-g21: ز before سودابه گفتار marks source/cause 'from / because of Sudabeh's speech'. گفتار is the noun 'speech / words'. باور کردن is the verb 'to believe'; here باور + نکرد (نکرد = negative past of کردن) yields 'he did not believe (it)'.
segments.s126-g22: نیامدش is an impersonal literary expression meaning 'it did not occur to him / it was not present to him' (the literal 'did not come to him'); زیشان 'from/of them' marks the group. کسی را introduces the subordinate idea 'that anyone...', with را as object/topic marker; بمرد here functions as a reported/embedded verb 'had died / that he died' (classical optative/subjunctive-form used in indirect statements), so the clause means 'it did not occur to him that any of them had died.'
segments.s126-g23: بشد is a classical past/optative form here conveying simple past/result 'he went / it came to pass' (literal senses include 'it happened / became'). با introduces the accompaniment 'with', followed by a coordinated plural: دلیران 'the brave' and کندآوران — an agentive plural; in context this names a group accompanying him (an archaic or compound epithet).
segments.s126-g24: به مهمانی is the goal 'to the banquet' (به = to; مهمانی = feast/banquet). شاه is 'the king' and هاماوران here names the place/people associated with the king (the king of Hamavaran); the whole phrase marks destination: 'to the king of Hamavaran's banquet.'
segments.s126-g113: نشستن is an infinitive/noun‑like verbal form here meaning 'to live/dwell' (a common poetic nominalization). به یک خانه با شهریار places the locative phrase after the verb: به + noun ('into/in a house') plus با شهریار ('with the king'). Word order is verb phrase → locative accompaniment, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s126-g114: پرستنده او بود is a simple past copular clause: noun ('devotee/worshipper') + pronoun object ('he/him') + past of بودن ('was'). و هم غمگسار appends a parallel predicate with و ('and') and هم ('also') before the noun 'consoler/companion in sorrow', so the structure is '[he] was X and also Y'.
segments.s126-g115: چو (when) introduces a temporal clause. بسته شد is a passive/perfective phrase meaning 'was bound/was subdued' (بسته here = 'bound/tied/sealed' and شد as past auxiliary), so the clause reads 'when that king was bound/subdued.' آن شاه دیهیم‌جوی is the topical noun phrase 'that avenging (demanding blood‑payment) king.'
segments.s126-g116: سپاهش = سپاه + ـش (possessive) 'his army/army of him'. به ایران نهادند روی is a standard classical phrasing: نهادند (they placed/put) + روی (face/direction) = 'they set/turned (its) face toward' and به ایران 'toward Iran'. The verb نهادند is past transitive 'they set/placed', used here idiomatically for 'directed'.
segments.s126-g117: پراگنده شد is a past intransitive construction 'became scattered/widespread' (پراگنده = scattered/widespread, شد = became). در جهان آگهی places the location/extent 'in the world' followed by the subject/complement 'news/announcement' (آگهی): literally 'an announcement became widespread in the world' = 'news spread throughout the world.'
segments.s126-g118: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. گم شد = 'was lost' or 'became lost' (گم 'lost' + شد 'became/was'); ز پالیز = 'from the orchard' (ز = from, poetic form of از); سرو سهی is a nominal phrase 'the dark/black cypress' (سروی poetic epithet سهی). The clause literal order is relative clause after آگهی: 'that [it] was lost from the orchard the dark cypress.'
segments.s126-g119: چو (when) again introduces a temporal clause. بر تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the golden throne' (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne, زرین = golden). ندیدند شاه is the negated past verb 'they did not see' with شاه as its object/topic: 'they did not see the king on the golden throne.' The verb is past tense with negative prefix ن‑.
segments.s126-g120: بجستن گرفتند is a compound action: بجستن (to seek/attempt) used as a verbal noun/infinitive notion, followed by گرفتند ('they took/seized') so the sense is 'they seized in order to seek' or idiomatically 'each seized his cap and sought (the throne)'. هر کس کلاه = 'each person (took) his hat/cap' (هر کس = every man, کلاه = cap). The line uses terse poetic order, but grammatically it's subject (هر کس) + object (کلاه) with گرفتند governing the action.
segments.s126-g33: به شهر اندر is a locative phrase meaning 'into/within the city' (classical word order puts the prepositional phrase before the comment). آوای رود و سرود is a coordinated noun phrase 'the voice/sound of the river and the song' (the genitive relation is implicit in classical style rather than marked by ezāfe). The whole segment sets the place and the sounds that will 'rise' in the next segment.
segments.s126-g34: به هم برکشیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb phrase: برکشیدند 'they raised/lifted up', with به هم ('to one another' / 'together') indicating they rose jointly. چون introduces a simile 'like', and تار و پود 'warp and weft' is the image — the simile follows the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s126-g35: چو here means 'when' and takes the verb immediately after it (typical classical/persian poetic order). دیدش is past tense 'saw' with the enclitic object -ش 'him'. سپهدار هاماوران is the subject 'the commander of (or commander from) Hamavaran' placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s126-g36: پیاده شدش is a verbal phrase meaning 'he became on foot' = 'he dismounted' (پیاده‌شدن = to dismount); classical Persian often uses شد + clitic to express such intransitive change. پیش 'forward/in front' (here 'came forward') and با مهتران 'with the chiefs' marks accompaniment; مهتران is plural 'chiefs/elders' (poetic/title form).
segments.s126-g37: ز ... تا expresses a range 'from ... to ...': ز ایوان سالار 'from the lord's iwan' تا پیش در 'as far as/before the gate'. Note classical order places the range-markers and place-words before the comment; پیش در functions as 'in front of the door/gate'.
segments.s126-g38: همه starts the following clause 'all/altogether' and introduces the list of precious things that 'rained' down. در و یاقوت بارید و زر is a poetic enumeration: items (pearls/pearls and rubies) 'rained' (بارید) and gold is added. (In classical Persian دُرّ 'pearl' is often written/treated as در in manuscripts; context shows these are gems falling like rain.)
segments.s126-g39: به زرین طبق‌ها is a directional phrase 'into/onto the golden platters' (the preposition phrase comes before the verb in poetic order). فروریختند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb from فرو ریختن 'to fall/pour (down)', used here to mean jewels/wealth 'poured' or 'fell' into the plates (the prefix فرو- intensifies the downward action).
segments.s126-g40: به سر ... همی بیختند: به سر = 'upon the head/top of' (here 'on top of the plates'), مشک و عنبر 'musk and amber(gris)' are the substances; همی is a classical/progressive particle marking continuous or repeated action (often translated 'continually' or 'then'), and بیختند is the past verb 'they poured/sprinkled' (verb form used transitively in context).
segments.s126-g153: کنون (adverb) sets the temporal frame 'now'. چاره‌ای is an indefinite accusative/nominative meaning 'a remedy/means'. باید is a modal auxiliary 'must/it is necessary'. انداختن is the infinitive 'to cast/bring about' placed after باید in a somewhat archaic/poetic order (modern Persian would more often put the finite verb after the object: باید چاره‌ای انداخت). Together they form a periphrasis meaning 'one must take a measure' or 'a remedy must be devised'.
segments.s126-g154: دل خویش is a possessive phrase: دل 'heart' + خویش (here written as خوی + ش) 'his/one's own'. ازین = از + ین (an older/poetic spelling of 'این') means 'from this'. رنج 'suffering' is the object of the following infinitive. پرداختن is an infinitive that here means 'to deal with/relieve' (classical usage can allow senses like 'attend to' or 'rid of'); it links back to the obligation in the previous segment: one must (باید) do this — namely, rid his heart of this suffering.
segments.s126-g155: ببارید is a past tense verb meaning 'fell/shone down' or 'shed' (used for drops/tears here). The sequence gives the subject (رستم) after the verb in the epic diction: 'Rostam shed/fell'. ز + چشم = 'from (his) eyes'. آب زرد is a descriptive compound 'yellow water' (poetic image for tears); the adjective زرد follows the noun it qualifies, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s126-g156: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessive ش). گشت is the past form of گشتن 'to become/turn', here 'became'. پرخون 'full of blood/bloodied' is a compound adjective predicate: دلش گشت پرخون 'his heart became blood-filled'. و links to the second coordinated clause: جان پر ز درد 'his life/soul full of pain' where پر 'full (of)' precedes the complement marked by ز. درد here carries the sense of sorrow/pain, so the two halves describe emotional and bodily torment.
segments.s126-g157: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic order meaning 'gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that is, saying that ...'). من با سپاه places the speaker and his accompaniment: من 'I', با 'with', سپاه 'the army' — i.e. 'I, together with the army'. Word order (verb later) is typical for reported speech in classical Persian.
segments.s126-g158: میان بسته‌ام is a perfect construction: میان 'the middle/among them' + بسته‌ام (بسته‌ام = 'I have closed/bound') meaning 'I have closed the middle' or 'I have formed a line in the middle' (i.e. deployed troops). جنگ را uses the object marker را marking جنگ 'the war/battle' as definite. کینه خواه is a compound where کینه 'hatred/grudge' + خواه (from خواستن 'to want') means 'seeking vengeance' — here functioning as the predicate 'I want the war for vengeance' or 'I seek war for revenge'. The overall clause asserts readiness to wage war motivated by vindictiveness.
segments.s126-g159: چو یابم is a temporal subordinate ('when I find/receive'); چو here = 'when' and یابم is a present/subjunctive form 'I find/obtain' used to introduce the conditional/time clause. ز + کاووس شاه = 'from King Kavus' (ز = from; کاووس شاه = King Kavus; the title follows the name). آگهی 'information/news' is the object: 'when I obtain news from King Kavus...'.
segments.s126-g160: کنم is the first-person present/future sense of کردن 'I will do/make' (used here to express a future action dependent on the previous 'when'). شهر ایران 'the city of Iran' is the patient/theme. ز ترکان تهی literally 'empty from the Turks' — ز = 'of/from', ترکان 'the Turks', and تهی 'empty/devoid'. The verb کنم takes this clause as its content: 'I will make the city of Iran empty of the Turks.'
segments.s126-g137: سپاه (subject) + اندر ایران (literary prepositional phrase 'in/within Iran') is followed by the predicate پراگنده شد. Here پراگنده is a predicative adjective 'scattered/widespread' and شد is the past/resultative verb 'became/was' marking a change of state. اندر is an archaic/poetic preposition equivalent to modern در/درون.
segments.s126-g138: A simple coordinated list زن و مرد و کودک ('women and men and children') is the topical noun phrase; همه is a distributive/emphatic modifier 'all/everyone' and بنده شد is the predicate 'became servants/slaves'. Note that شد is singular here though همه refers collectively — a common idiom in older Persian where the predicate is treated as a unit.
segments.s126-g139: همه is the subject 'everyone'. در گرفتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase ('to take to the house/doors', i.e. to shut oneself up or take refuge). ز ایران پناه should be read together as 'sought refuge in/throughout Iran'—ز here marks the relation with پناه in poetic diction (roughly 'for/from/within Iran' used to indicate the locus of refuge). گرفتند is past plural 'they took'.
segments.s126-g140: به ایرانیان is a dative phrase 'for/to the Iranians'. گشت is the past/resultative 'became/turned' and governs the predicative phrase گیتی سیاه 'the world black' (i.e. 'the world became black' — a metaphor for gloom/despair). سیاه here carries the sense 'dark/gloomy' rather than literal color.
segments.s126-g141: دو بهره is a quantifying nominal phrase 'two shares/units/divisions' (بهره used here as a military or distributive unit). سوی is the preposition 'toward', زاولستان is a place name, and شدند is the past plural copula/verb 'they went/became' indicating movement or change of station.
segments.s126-g142: به خواهش means 'by/at the request (of someone)'. بر + personal name (بر پور دستان شدند) expresses attachment or placement 'they came to/placed themselves with Pour‑Dastan'; شدند is the past plural 'they became/were'. Here پور دستان is a proper name (پور as the name element and دستان as the following name element).
segments.s126-g143: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'that/so that'. ما را is the beneficiary/object 'for us', ز بدها = 'from evils/misfortunes', and تو باشی پناه is a predicative optative/subjunctive: تو (you) + باشی (second‑person present/subjunctive/optative 'may you be' or 'be thou') + پناه (refuge). The construction expresses a plea/request that 'you be a refuge for us against evils'.
segments.s126-g144: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گم شد is the perfective passive/anticausative 'was lost'. سر تاج literally 'the head/top of the crown' (an expression for the crown/sovereignty) is the thing lost, and کاووس شاه is the name with title 'Kavus the king' (name + title in apposition). Word order places the temporal clause after the plea, giving the reason/occasion.
segments.s126-g105: The clause is a reported speech formula: the quoted content 'جدایی نخواهم ز کاووس' (“I will not be separated from Kavus”) comes before the reporting verb 'گفت' (“he said”), which is placed at the end. 'نخواهم' is the first‑person negative present/future of خواستن and here expresses refusal/will ('I will not'); the preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') marks origin/source ('from Kavus').
segments.s126-g106: 'وگرچه' introduces a concessive clause ('even though' / 'although'). 'لحد باشد' uses باد (باشد) in a subjunctive/optative sense ('should be / may be a grave'); word order places the noun 'لحد' before the verb. 'او را' is the object with the enclitic object marker 'را', and 'نهفت' functions as a verb/adjectival predicate 'conceals / hides' — together: 'even if a tomb should hide him.'
segments.s126-g107: 'چو' here introduces a conditional 'if/when'. 'کاووس را' is the patient marked by the object marker 'را'. 'بند' means 'fetter/captivity', and the impersonal construction 'باید + کشید' expresses necessity/passive sense: 'it must be put in bonds' or 'Kavus must be bound.' Note the classical/poetic impersonal phrasing rather than a simple active clause.
segments.s126-g108: This clause states the painful consequence with an impersonal necessity: 'مرا' (me / my) plus object marker 'را' marks the affected person; 'بی‌گنه سر' = 'innocent head' (adjective before noun in poetry). 'بباید برید' is 'it must be cut' — 'بباید' (necessity) + past stem 'برید' gives the sense 'must be severed' (i.e., beheaded). The construction thus means 'my innocent head must be cut off.'
segments.s126-g109: 'بگفتند' is the reporting verb ('they said' / 'it was said'), introducing what follows. 'گفتار او با پدر' is a noun phrase 'his speech/words with his father' — subject/content of the reported statement. Word order is straightforward: reporting verb + noun phrase.
segments.s126-g110: This is a compact descriptive clause: 'پر از کین' = 'full of hatred', and 'شدش' = 'it became his / befell him' where '-ش' is the enclitic pronoun referring to him (i.e., 'he became filled'). 'سر' (head) is the locus: 'سر (شدش) پر از کین' — 'his head became full of hatred.' The second half 'پر از خون جگر' repeats the 'full of' pattern: 'full of the blood of the jigar/heart' — a poetic idiom for deep grief. Note the archaic clitic on 'شد' and the nominal+prepositional 'پر از ...' construction.
segments.s126-g111: 'به حصنش فرستاد' = 'he sent to his fortress' where 'حصنش' includes the possessive clitic '-ش' ('his fortress'). The following 'نزدیک شوی' is a subordinate (purpose/result) clause using the subjunctive 'شوی' of شدن ('become') — literally 'that he may be near'. Poetic word order places the purpose clause after the main verb.
segments.s126-g112: Two adjectival/nominal phrases describe the person sent: 'جگر خسته از غم' = 'heart/liver tired from grief' (jigar as the seat of feeling), using the pattern Noun + adjective + 'از' phrase; and 'به خون شسته روی' = 'face washed with blood', where 'به' marks the means/instrument and 'شسته' (washed) is a past participle used adjectivally. Together they portray him as grief‑worn and blood‑stained in the face.
segments.s126-g9: بدین‌گونه (adverb) opens the clause meaning 'thus'; با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him'; همی is a poetic preverbal particle here indicating continued or emphatic action; چاره (object) precedes the verb in the SOV order typical of classical Persian; جست is the past form of جستن 'to seek'. The whole segment reads literally 'thus with him always sought (a) remedy.'
segments.s126-g10: نهان بند functions as a poetic noun phrase meaning a 'secret design/plan' (نهان = hidden; بند used here as 'bond/plan' in a figurative sense); او is the subject/pronoun 'his' or 'he'; بود is the past copula 'was'; رایش درست is a predicative nominal ('رایش' = his counsel/opinion, درست = correct). The clause therefore says 'his secret plan was: his counsel was correct.'
segments.s126-g11: مگر here introduces the exception/conditional sense 'unless'; شهر و دختر is a coordinated subject 'the city and the maiden'; بماند is the present/subjunctive form of ماندن 'remain' used here in the conditional sense; بدوی (literally 'original/primitive') is used adjectivally/adverbially to mean 'as originally' or 'as before'.
segments.s126-g12: نباشدش is the negative present/subjunctive form نباشد with enclitic -ش ('for him/it'), giving the idea 'it would not be (for him)'; بر سر is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over (one's) head' and یکی باژجوی is 'one extortioner' or 'a single demanding claimant'. Together: 'there would not be for him even one extortioner upon his head.'
segments.s126-g13: بدانست is the simple past 'she knew/realized'; in classical poetic word order the verb can precede the subject, so 'بدانست سودابه' = 'Sūdābeh realized'; رای پدر is the object phrase 'the father's counsel/opinion'.
segments.s126-g14: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that'; با سور is a prepositional phrase 'at/with the feast' (سور = banquet/feast here); پرخاش دارد is a present-tense verb phrase 'has reproach/acts aggressively' (دارد used as main verb 'has' or 'shows'); به سر is an adverbial 'upon the head' used idiomatically to mean 'toward/against (someone)'. The clause describes the content of the father's counsel: that he will be aggressive/quarrelsome at the feast.
segments.s126-g15: به + proper name (به کاووس) is a dative 'to Kāvus'; کی here is an interrogative 'who' (poetic word-order places the interrogative before یا after the verb); گفت is the past 'said/told' (poetically placed after the dative and interrogative); کاین رای نیست is a subordinate clause 'that this counsel is not (so)'. The whole is a rhetorical question: 'Who told Kāvus that this counsel is not [valid]?'
segments.s126-g16: ترا is the object 'you' formed from the 2nd-person suffix (ت) plus the object marker (را); خود is emphatic 'yourself'; به هاماوران is a locative 'to/at Hamāvarān'; جای نیست is an idiom 'there is no place/room' meaning 'you have no standing/place (there)'. Grammatically: 'ترا خود' is emphatic direct object and جای نیست is the verbless predicate 'is no place (for you)'.
